sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Steven, has been experiencing chronic back pain for approximately two years, which started after a fall onto his back during a basketball game.
The pain is sharp and throbbing, and he rates it a 7 out of 10.
The pain radiates down his left leg, and he experiences numbness in his toes.
He has also experienced incontinence once, which he attributes to standing for an extended period and drinking too much.
He has tried physical therapy and injections in the past, but they were not effective.
He has gained 80 pounds in the past year and is relatively inactive, sitting at home most of the time.
Past medical history: The patient has a distant family history of scoliosis, with his fourth cousin affected.
He has a history of smoking, but has not smoked in a while.
Review of systems: The patient denies any fever, chills, or weight loss.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has midline tenderness on the L4-L5 disk space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness when bending forward.
The pain is exacerbated by lumbar flexion and extension.
The straight leg test is positive.
There is decreased sensation to light touch at the right lateral thigh at L4 dermatome.
The patellar and Achilles reflexes are symmetrical.
Test Results: The MRI results show a disk desiccation, a disk bulge with paracentral disk herniation resulting in moderate right foraminal stenosis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a herniated disk at the L4-L5 level with radiculopathy, which is causing chronic lumbar back pain and radiating pain and numbness in the left leg.
Problem list: Herniated disk at L4-L5 level; chronic lumbar back pain; radiculopathy.
Plan:
I prescribed a spinal injection with a corticosteroid to help alleviate the inflammation and directly target the bulge causing the patient's pain.
I will write a prescription for a medication to help calm the patient's anxiety before the procedure.
I will also schedule the injection to be performed under fluoroscopy.
If the patient continues to experience pain after the injection, we will explore pain medication options, but I would like to try the steroid shot as a first line of treatment.
If the patient still experiences pain after the injection, we will consider surgery.
Follow-up: The patient will schedule the spinal injection with the front desk, which should take about an hour.
If the procedure is successful, we may only need to do it four times a year.
